texts.orig: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane-based technology has been recognized as one of the most important separation processes because of its energy e ffi ciency [1 -3], good selectivity, reasonable cost [4], easy control [5,6], lower energy consumption due to no phase change, suitability for use on o ff shore platforms, and an easy scale-up owing to its modular design [7 -9]. Thus, the membrane process is a promising technology for improving the quality of natural gas by sequestering water, H2S, and CO2 gases from natural gas. These impurities must be removed before the gas is delivered to a pipeline, to prevent corrosion of the pipeline and to improve the gas calori fi c value. Hollow- fi ber membranes are the best form of membranes for use in gas separation processes because they can be produced by any method employed for the manufacture of chemical fi bers [10], and they are the cheapest membranes on a per square meter basis; they have the highest membrane area to the module volume ratio compared with other membrane designs such as plate-and-frame or spiral wound modules [11].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A dynamic simulation model of a hollow fi ber membrane module has been developed by deriving new transient mole fraction balances, energy balances, and gas velocity equations based on mole balances. The adopted fl ow type of the membrane module is a counter-current fl ow for the recovery of methane from coalbed methane (CBM), which is a form of natural gas extracted from coalbeds and is composed of mainly methane (up to 80 -99 vol%) and minor impurities (CO2, N2, etc.). This study assumes that the CBM feed gas for the dynamic simulation consists of 90% methane and 10% carbon dioxide. In the simulation model, CBM feed gas is supplied into the bore side feed, and puri fi ed methane rich gas, the retentate gas, is produced from the product side of the bores. Carbon dioxide rich gas, the permeate gas, is discharged from the outlet end of the shell side. The simulation results are very similar to the experimental data of Airrane Co., Ltd. in Korea. This paper presents the e ff ects of design and operating conditions on the performance, and investigates the transient behaviors of membrane modules using the validated simulation model.

Kovvali et al. solved the multicomponent gas permeator transport equations considering the permeate pressure e ff ect and reduced computational e ff orts by using a linear assumption of the local compositions between the bore and shell sides [11]. Petterson and Lien used an algebraic model to theoretically investigate the inherent behavior of several single-stage and multi-stage permeator systems, without considering the permeate side pressure build-up when separating a binary gas mixture [15]. Thundyil and Koros suggested a new approach based on a fi nite element called the ' Succession of States method ' to solve the mass transfer problem of the permeation process in a hollow fi ber permeator [16]. Orthogonal collocation methods were adopted to solve the di ff erential equations of membrane models by Tessendorf et al. [17] and Kaldis et al. [18]. texts.orig: Although many studies have been carried out on the modeling and simulation of membrane systems, there have been few works on the dynamic simulation of membrane models calculating transient gas velocities, mole fractions, molar fl ow rates, etc. in the permeate stream (shell side) and the retentate stream (bore side), simultaneously, to the best of the author's knowledge. A dynamic simulation model is crucial for the rational design of gas separation membrane modules because a dynamic model can predict transient behaviors at startup and shutdown, as well as variations a ff ected by unexpected disturbances.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study introduces a new dynamic simulation model of a boreside feed hollow fi ber membrane module by deriving new mole fraction balances, energy balances, gas velocity equations, etc. The target feed gas is coalbed methane (CBM) and the feed composition is assumed as 90% methane and 10% carbon dioxide. The hollow fi ber membrane module produces a puri fi ed methane gas and a carbon dioxide-rich gas. The simulation model has been validated by comparing the simulation results with experimental data. Section 2 explains the current dynamic simulation model developed using a gPROMS custom modeling system (ver. 1.0.0) [28]. Section 3 presents the results of the model validation and investigations of sensitivity analysis and dynamic behaviors. Section 4 concludes this work.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Formulation of target process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hollow fi ber modules containing thousands of fi bers are of interest in large-scale industrial applications and are desirable con fi gurations in view of their overall performance and economic feasibility, their high membrane area to module volume ratio (> 1000 m 2 /m 3 ) that results in high productivity per volume unit and cost-e ffi cient production [29,30], and their self-supporting feature [30]. Hence, a hollow fi ber membrane module for the CBM puri fi cation has been adopted in this study. In this work, the new dynamic simulation model of the bore side feed hollow fi ber membrane module with a counter-current fl ow pattern has been developed. The CBM feed gas, assumed to be a gas mixture of 90% CH4 and 10% CO2, enters the bore sides, and puri fi ed CH4, referred to as the retentate gas, is obtained at the product end of the bore sides. A CO2-rich gas, called the permeate gas, is obtained at the outlet end of the shell side.

texts.orig: This study introduces a new dynamic model for hollow fi ber membranes including transient mole fraction balances, energy balances, molar fl ow rates, etc. in an axial direction. The transient molar fl ow rate variables can be easily converted into the gas velocity variables in the axial domain. As the precise calculation of the gas velocities, as well as the molar fl ow rates, directly a ff ects the prediction accuracy of the membrane performance, such as the methane purity and recovery, the exact formulation of the dynamic equations including the molar fl ow rates and the gas velocities is of great importance. However, there seem to be few works simultaneously calculating the transient gas velocities, molar fl ow rates, concentrations, and temperatures through dynamic simulation modeling of membranes; the methods used to calculate the dynamic gas velocities of the retentate and permeate streams within the membrane module have not been clearly explained in previous literature [25 -27].

texts.orig: 3. Results of dynamic simulation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The basic design conditions for the membrane dynamic simulation are shown in Table 2. To conduct the dynamic simulation, the permeance parameters of methane and carbon dioxide have been estimated. Validation of the developed simulation model was achieved by comparison with the experimental data of Airrane Co., Ltd., a membrane vendor company in Korea.
texts.text: The basic design conditions for the membrane dynamic simulation are shown in Table 2. To conduct the dynamic simulation, the permeance parameters of methane and carbon dioxide have been estimated. Validation of the developed simulation model was achieved by comparison with the experimental data of Airrane Co., Ltd., a membrane vendor company in Korea.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As can be seen in Tables 3 and 4, the simulation results show good agreement with the experimental data under di ff erent operating conditions, such as various feed fl ow rates and stage cuts, when the difference between the feed pressure and permeate pressure is 0.5 MPa or 0.7 MPa. There are very small relative mean errors (RMEs) between the predicted values and the measured data of each variable, such as the product fl ow rate, the waste fl ow rate, the methane mole fractions of the product and waste streams, and the methane purity and recovery. Hence, the developed simulation model is judged to be accurate enough to predict membrane performances. The RME is de fi ned as Eq. (48):

texts.orig: This study also investigates the dynamic behaviors according to time-varying conditions such as feed composition and feed fl ow rate. Fig. 10 and Fig. 11 show the methane purity and recovery pro fi les with the time when the methane mole fraction in the feed gas and the feed fl ow are varied, respectively. texts.orig: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper introduces a new dynamic membrane model using the gPROMS custom modeling software [28]. A hollow fi ber membrane with a counter-current fl ow con fi guration has been adopted because this is regarded as a highly desirable membrane system for the puri fi cation of methane gas by the removal of carbon dioxide from CBM gas.
texts.text: This paper introduces a new dynamic membrane model using the gPROMS custom modeling software [28]. A hollow fi ber membrane with a counter-current fl ow con fi guration has been adopted because this is regarded as a highly desirable membrane system for the puri fi cation of methane gas by the removal of carbon dioxide from CBM gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The simulation results were well validated by comparison with experimental data provided by the membrane vendor company, Airrane Co., Ltd. This dynamic model is signi fi cant in its ability to predict, simultaneously, the transient behaviors of mole fractions, gas velocities, pressure pro fi le, etc. as well as the membrane performance such as methane purity and recovery.

texts.orig: This model can be extended to simulate a multi-component (CH4, CO2, and N2) separation system as shown in Table A1 in the Appendix. The temperature e ff ects on the performance are shown in Fig. A1 in the Appendix. However, this model may be limited in its ability to accurately predict the performances a ff ected by the di ff erent temperature conditions, because the permeance ( QCH4, QCO2, QN2 ) values of each component were estimated only at 298.15 K and are assumed to be constant in this study. To obtain a more precise prediction of the temperature e ff ects on performance, the permeance values must be estimated using experimental results under at least three di ff erent temperature conditions and the permeance parameters need to be expressed as functions of the temperature, if the temperature has a strong in fl uence on the permeance.
